title: Mission & Vision
description: The mission and vision that guide Boys and Girls High School.
body:
Who we are

Our Mission

The mission of Boys and Girls High School is to prepare our students to achieve their full potential, make positive choices, and have a plan for life after high school. This will be accomplished by creating an environment that will successfully teach our students to think critically and conscientiously about themselves, their community, and their global village.

Our Vision

We believe students achieve success and embrace learning when they feel safe and are supported by competent and caring adults.

Boys and Girls High School — Home of the Kangaroos — serves the Bedford-Stuyvesant community at 1700 Fulton Street, Brooklyn, NY 11213.

title: Principal's Message
description: A welcome message from Dr. Grecian Harrison, Principal of Boys and Girls High School.
body:
Dear Scholars,

I pray that you are well, safe, and ready to begin another school year. I pray that this will be your most active and successful school-year. I pray that you continue on the road to success, happiness, and discovery. For some, I pray that you discover your self-worth.

At Boys and Girls High School, our mission is to help to prepare you for life after high school. Because there are many roads one may take, you will have to make the choice. Do you choose roads that lead to success, or do you choose roads that lead to failure. Contrary to what some may have told you, failure is an option — one that I hope you do not choose.

Where do you fit? When you develop your ability to think, you improve the quality of the choices you make. Your choices impact your quality of life. I hope you choose peace over violence; hard work over procrastination; love over hate; maturity over immaturity; scholarship over ignorance; success over failure.

There are no limits on your ability to achieve except for those you place on yourself. I believe in you. However, it is more important that you believe in yourself. Stay strong!

Sincerely,
Grecian Harrison, Ed.D.
Principal

title: Our History
description: The story of Boys High School, Girls High School, and the historic institution they became.
body:
Editor note: this page was "Coming Soon!" on the original site. Replace this note with real content — edit this file directly or via tools/build.py.

Boys and Girls High School traces its roots to Brooklyn's original Boys High School and Girls High School — institutions whose alumni include Congresswoman Shirley Chisholm, entertainer and activist Lena Horne, and author Isaac Asimov. Known across Bedford-Stuyvesant simply as "The High," the school has stood at 1700 Fulton Street as a pillar of the community.

Meet our noted alumni

title: School Leadership Team
description: What the School Leadership Team does and when it meets.
body:
What is a School Leadership Team?

School Leadership Teams (SLTs) are vehicles for developing school-based educational policies, and ensuring that resources are aligned to implement those policies. SLTs assist in the evaluation and assessment of a school's educational programs and their effects on student achievement. SLTs play a significant role in creating a structure for school-based decision making, and shaping the path to a collaborative school culture.

New York State Education Law Section 2590-h requires every New York City public school to have a School Leadership Team. Chancellor's Regulation A-655 establishes guidelines to ensure the formation of effective SLTs in every school.

What is the role of an SLT?

An SLT is responsible for developing a school's Comprehensive Educational Plan (CEP). The SLT is not responsible for the hiring or firing of school staff.

Meeting dates

SLT meetings take place on the 3rd Tuesday of each month. Dates are announced on the school calendar and are subject to change.

title: FAQs
description: Answers to the questions families ask most about credits, Regents, attendance, and counseling.
body:
If students fail a course, can they still take the Regents exam in that subject?▾

Yes. However, passing the Regents exam does not guarantee that the student will pass the course.

How can I keep track of my child's progress?▾

Sign up for PupilPath. Contact Ms. Gaston, our Parent Coordinator, at (718) 467-1700 ext. 1071 for additional information.

Does attendance affect course grades?▾

Yes. Students are expected to achieve and maintain a daily attendance rate of 92% or better throughout the year.

How many Regents exams are needed to graduate?▾

Students must pass 5 Regents in specific areas for a Regents diploma. Students interested in an Advanced Regents Diploma must pass 9 Regents in specific areas.

What is the role of the guidance counselor?▾

Guidance counselors help navigate students through high school with counseling and support for academic, social, and emotional needs. They schedule courses for day school, Extended Day (Saturday, iCourse), and Summer School, and guide students through college and career preparation. Parents should maintain ongoing communication with counselors throughout the year and during Parent-Teacher Conferences. Counselors are identified on program cards and report cards.

How is high school different from elementary and middle school?▾

High school courses earn credit for each passed course — typically one credit per term per passed course.

What is the minimum passing grade?▾

65%. Higher grades lead to more options for college admissions, scholarships, and future employment — students should aim for the highest grades possible.

How many credits are needed for promotion?▾

To be promoted to: 10th grade — 11 credits; 11th grade — 22 credits (including 4 in English and/or ESL and 4 in Social Studies); 12th grade — 33 credits.

How many credits do students need to graduate?▾

All students need 44+ credits in required subject areas (CTE and IEP diplomas differ). See Graduation Requirements.

Is Physical Education required?▾

Yes. Students must accumulate 4 credits by passing PE during 7 terms. A passing grade in PE is awarded approximately .58 credits (7 PE courses × .58 credits = 4.06 credits).

Academic departments

Boys and Girls High School offers a full college-preparatory curriculum along with Career and Technical Education (CTE) programs including Electrical Installation and Architecture Design, Advanced Placement courses, and STEAM programs.

Support departments

Counseling in Schools

Every student is assigned a Success Coach; a Mental Health Counselor is on site. Learn more

Editor note: the full department directory (teachers per department) lives in the Edlio "Departments" app on the original site and should be exported and pasted here before launch.

title: Academic Programs
description: Advanced Placement, Regents Preparation, Saturday and P.M. School, YABC, and CTE Site Safety Training.
body:
Advanced Placement▾

BGHS offers 5 Advanced Placement courses. Ask your counselor about AP enrollment for the coming term.

Regents Preparation▾

Prepare with these resources:

  • Castle Learning Online
  • Khan Academy
  • NYS Regents website (past exams)
  • Regents Review

Saturday Regents Boot Camp runs each spring — see news for the current schedule.

Saturday School / P.M. School▾

Extended-day and weekend options for credit recovery and acceleration. Contact your counselor to enroll.

YABC — Young Adult Borough Center▾

Evening program supporting students who are behind in credit toward earning their diploma.

NYC Department of Buildings — Site Safety Training (CTE)▾

BGHS is a Site Safety Training (SST) Course Provider under NYC Department of Buildings Local Law (1 RCNY §105-03). Two SST courses are offered — 8-Hour Fall Prevention and 2-Hour Drug and Alcohol Awareness (10 hours total) — provided to current students in the Electrical Installation and Practice CTE program during senior year by instructor Stephen Hudson ((718) 467-1700 ext. 3700).

title: Graduation Requirements
description: Credits, Regents exams, and promotion requirements for a BGHS diploma.
body:
Credits

Credit requirements by milestone
MilestoneCredits required
Promotion to 10th grade11 credits
Promotion to 11th grade22 credits (incl. 4 English/ESL + 4 Social Studies)
Promotion to 12th grade33 credits
Graduation44+ credits in required subject areas (CTE and IEP diplomas differ)

Regents exams

Students must pass 5 Regents in specific areas for a Regents diploma, or 9 Regents for an Advanced Regents Diploma. Physical Education: 4 credits accumulated across 7 terms.

Questions? See the FAQs or ask your counselor.

title: In-School Guest Wi-Fi
description: How DOE devices connect to the nycdoeMobile wireless network.
body:
The DOE has enabled a wireless network ID in all school buildings (nycdoeMobile). All centrally-loaned DOE iPads automatically connect to the nycdoeMobile Wi-Fi network inside DOE school buildings:

  • No additional setup is required for DOE iPads to connect; every centrally-loaned iPad is configured to connect automatically.
  • All other devices should connect to the school's existing Wi-Fi network.
  • If a centrally-loaned iPad does not connect automatically, submit a Help Desk ticket (have the device serial number handy): SupportHub or (718) 935-5100.

title: College & Career Corner
description: College counseling, applications, financial aid, and career readiness at BGHS.
body:
The College & Career Center

The center is led by school counselor Jeannelle Louis, who publicizes and hosts events for all students and provides opportunities for life after high school. Services include:

  • Catalogues from two- and four-year colleges, vocational and trade schools throughout the United States (online information also available)
  • College counseling — request an appointment through your official teacher
  • Appointments with visiting college representatives and College Fair information
  • SAT applications and test dates for the year
  • Help completing applications, financial aid (FAFSA, EOP, HEOP, TAP, PELL), and scholarships (POSSE, UFT, and more)

College readiness

The DOE's College and Career Readiness Benchmarks define what students need to enroll, persist, and succeed in college, postsecondary training, and meaningful careers.

Helpful links

FAFSA

The free federal aid form every current and prospective college student should file annually.

HESC / TAP

New York State's Tuition Assistance Program — uses your FAFSA for the online TAP application.

CUNY

High-quality, accessible education at 24 campuses across New York City.

SUNY

One of the largest comprehensive public university systems in the country.

title: Safety & Reporting
description: Child abuse prevention and mandated reporting under Chancellor's Regulation A-750.
body:
Child abuse prevention — highlights of Chancellor's Regulation A-750

If you have reasonable cause to suspect child abuse or maltreatment:

  • A mandated reporter must personally make the report by calling the State Central Register (SCR) at 1 (800) 635-1522, immediately, and obtain a "Call I.D." number.
  • The mandated reporter must identify the name, title, and contact information of all school officials with direct knowledge of the allegations.
  • Inform the Principal or designee of the allegations and the Call I.D. number.
  • Submit the written report (LDSS-2221-A) within 48 hours to the Administration for Children's Services (ACS) in the borough where the alleged subject lives.

title: Parent Coordinator
description: A message from Ms. LaVonne Gaston, BGHS Parent Coordinator.
body:
My name is Ms. LaVonne Gaston, Parent Coordinator. I am so honored and proud for the role I have in your family's success here at Boys and Girls High School. It is indeed a privilege for me to support, encourage and develop positive relationships between families and our school.

Here at Boys and Girls High School we strongly encourage the involvement of our parents and guardians. We realize that you are our most valued stakeholder. So please, make yourselves right at home. Your thoughts, opinions and involvement are very important and you are welcomed here. #OURPARENTSMATTER.

As your Parent Coordinator, I support the vision and the goals that our Principal has for our students and their families. One of our goals this year is to increase parent involvement to 65%. I know TOGETHER we can do it. This is going to be an exciting year, and I look forward to working closely with you and your family.

Please feel free to contact me by telephone at (718) 467-1700 extension 1071 or 1073. I am here for you.

title: PTCA
description: The Parent, Teacher & Community Association: membership, board, and monthly meeting dates.
body:
Parent, Teacher & Community Associations (PTCAs) are school-based organizations. All parents are automatically members of their school's PTCA — no pre-qualifications required. "Parent" includes a student's parent or guardian, or any person in a parental or custodial relationship to the student.

For more information on getting involved, ask our Principal, Dr. Grecian Harrison, or our Parent Coordinator, Ms. LaVonne Gaston.

Meetings

PTA meetings: 2nd Tuesday of each month · SLT meetings: 3rd Tuesday of each month. Meetings are subject to change — check news for each month's notice.

Editor note: the executive board list from the original site is from 2022–23. Update after this year's elections (see June PTA meeting notices).

title: Parents' Bill of Rights
description: NYC parents' rights and responsibilities, including translated versions.
body:
Each child's maximum potential can best be achieved through a partnership between parents and the education community. To foster active engagement, parents have certain rights and responsibilities. Parents have the right to:

  • A free public school education for their child, from kindergarten until age 21 or receipt of a high school diploma, whichever comes first, in a safe and supportive learning environment free from discrimination, harassment, bullying, and bigotry
  • An evaluation for their child with a disability and, if found in need of special education, a free, appropriate education from age 3 through 21
  • Bilingual education or English as a Second Language services for their child with limited English proficiency
  • Their child's full instructional schedule in accordance with the DOE school year calendar

Read the full Bill of Rights on schools.nyc.gov

Translations

PDF

Parents' Bill of Rights (Polish)

Polish translation. All other languages are available on schools.nyc.gov.

title: Counseling & Support
description: Counseling in Schools partnership, Success Coaches, and BGHS school counselors.
body:
Counseling in Schools

Counseling in Schools goes where children, youth, and families face the greatest challenges and builds on their strengths to help them gain skills for success. At BGHS:

  • Each student is assigned a Success Coach supporting them and their families through all aspects of school
  • A Mental Health Counselor is on site for therapeutic counseling and family support
  • The After School program provides additional academic and social support
  • Individual and group counseling

School counselors

  • Jeannelle Louis — School Counselor, College & Career Center
  • Damali Jimenez-Ozoria — School Counselor, Cohort Z (Class of 2024) & Cohort 3 (Class of 2027) — (718) 467-1700 ext. 4011

Email addresses are protected on the public site; reach counselors through the staff directory or the main office.

title: Grief Support
description: Understanding grief, plus resources and staff support for students and families.
body:
What is grief?

"Grief is the natural reaction to loss. Grief is both a universal and a personal experience. Some examples of loss include the death of a loved one, the ending of an important relationship, job loss, loss through theft or the loss of independence through disability." — Mayo Clinic

The five stages — denial, anger, bargaining, depression, and acceptance — are part of the framework of learning to live with loss. Not everyone goes through all of these steps in the same order; they are tools to help us understand what we may be feeling.

BGHS has a committed staff that can provide support for you and your family whenever you need it — contact us through the main office or the counseling team.

Resources

  • What's Your Grief? — practical articles on coping
  • Grief.com — the five stages of grief
  • Benefits of grief journaling; "Ball in the Box" analogy — ask our counselors for printed copies

title: How to Apply
description: How to apply to Boys and Girls High School through NYC's high school admissions process.
body:
Join the Kangaroo family

Thank you for your interest in Boys and Girls High School

New York City has the most extensive system of school choice in the country. Everyone must apply to high school. BGHS offers an array of academic courses, support programs, school activities, college accreditation, Career and Technical Education programs, and so much more.

Getting started▾

The year-long admissions process begins at the end of 7th grade with the High School Admissions Guide, which explains the DOE's MySchools directory of 400+ schools. Families typically research options in the fall of 8th grade through open houses, tours, and borough high school fairs each September and October.

Filling out your application▾

Students complete the citywide high school application online in their MySchools account, ranking up to 12 schools in order of preference. The deadline is typically early December; placements arrive in March. We accept everyone who lives in the attendance zone or is interested in our school and its options.

Tips: be sure you're eligible for a school before listing it, and don't list a school you're not willing to attend.

Moving to NYC mid-year or over the summer▾

Reach out to a Family Welcome Center to find a seat. It helps to call schools directly first — including ours — to check admissions criteria and space, then go to the Welcome Center with a list of schools that fit your child.

What to consider▾

The commute: try the trip before you rank us. Small school or large? We offer the benefits of both. Fast-track or laid-back? We blend learning styles with a true college-prep curriculum. "Chalk & talk" or collaborative? Our diverse teaching community offers both.

Your options: CTE and Early College▾

Career and Technical Education (CTE): traditional coursework plus professional training — our programs include Electrical Engineering and Architecture Design, with more in development. Students graduate college- and job-ready, earning professional certifications.

Early College: combine high school with up to two years of free college credit at a CUNY partner campus through the Early College Initiative.

Take the tour

VID

BGHS Open House & Orientation Tour

See our classrooms, programs, and student life — right here on this page.

Enrollment contacts

  • Kimberly Henry-Rerrie — Assistant Principal
  • Alica Martin — Assistant Principal
  • LaVonne Gaston — Parent Coordinator, (718) 467-1700 ext. 1071
  • Jeannelle Louis & Damali Jimenez-Ozoria — School Counselors

Request transcripts / records

title: Noted Alumni
description: Shirley Chisholm, Lena Horne, Isaac Asimov, and the legacy of Boys and Girls High School.
body:
Boys High School and Girls High School — the historic institutions that became BGHS — produced some of America's most consequential figures.

1924–2005

Shirley Chisholm

American politician, educator, and author. First African-American woman elected to Congress (1969–1983, NY's 12th District) and, in 1972, the first major-party Black candidate for President of the United States and first woman to seek the Democratic nomination. Alumna of Girls' High School.

1917–2010

Lena Horne

Singer, actress, dancer, and civil rights activist born in Bedford-Stuyvesant. Attended Girls High School, the all-girls public high school that became Boys and Girls High School.

1920–1992

Isaac Asimov

Author and professor of biochemistry, among the most prolific writers of all time with more than 500 books of science fiction and popular science. Attended Boys High School, graduating at 15.
